Skill Demand and Supply in Poland 2025

Insights from Online Job Postings and Trainings

Published

April 14, 2026

Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This report analyses labour demand and supply in Poland through the lens of online job advertisements and training provision data. It combines web-scraped vacancy data from Pracuj.pl, the largest online job portal in Poland, with information from the Baza Usług Rozwojowych (BUR), the country’s main database of training and upskilling services. The analysis focuses on how employers signal required skills and how these relate to the skills offered through the training system in 2025.

The study integrates labour market analysis with modern NLP methods, including automated text processing, classification, and LLM-assisted skill extraction, to transform unstructured job postings and training descriptions into comparable measures of skill demand and skill supply.

Using mappings to two skill taxonomies, ESCO and Lightcast, the report compares the composition of requested and supplied skills across occupations, with particular attention to digital competencies and emerging skill needs. It also introduces indicators of relative labour demand, derived from job posting intensity, and contrasts them with the structure of available training.

The Polish labour market is undergoing rapid transformation, with increasing digitalization shaping both hiring practices and skill development pathways. Online job postings provide a timely proxy for employer demand, while training data from BUR offers insight into the supply of skills and reskilling opportunities, enabling a joint assessment of potential mismatches between demand and supply.

Regional Coverage

Relative labour demand

The map shows regional demand and training density normalized by the average labour force in 2025. Use the switch above the map to change the view between job offers and BUR training services.

Regional comparison table

Counts are shown alongside their labour-force-normalized counterparts to make regional demand and supply comparable across differently sized voivodeships. Click a column header to sort the table.

Quick read

Key regional patterns

Data and Methodology

Data collection

We collect job advertisements from Pracuj.pl, the largest online job portal in Poland, and training-service listings from Baza Usług Rozwojowych, the country’s main public database of training and upskilling services. The analytical sample shown in this report focuses on records that can be assigned to Polish voivodeships and linked to the regional demand and supply comparison.

For job advertisements, we retain records with sufficient textual content to support downstream NLP processing, including job titles and descriptions. For BUR, we use training-service records with usable regional metadata and service descriptions. We remove duplicates and standardize core fields before the records enter the analytical pipeline.

Processing pipeline

Raw job postings and training descriptions contain noisy, unstructured information. We transform them into a standardized analytic dataset in six steps: we first identify the source language, translate non-Polish text into Polish, isolate skill-relevant sentences, map extracted skills to the ESCO and Lightcast skill taxonomies, standardize job titles to ESCO occupations using both titles and skill context for disambiguation, and then enrich each record with regional information and labour-force denominators for voivodeship-level comparison.

01
Language
Detection
Automatic identification of the source language for each job posting or training description
02
Translation
Non-Polish descriptions are translated into Polish before downstream NLP processing
03
Skill-Sentence
Filtering
Skill-relevant fragments are identified in vacancy and training text before structured extraction
04
Skill
Taxonomy Mapping
Extracted skills are mapped to the ESCO and Lightcast taxonomies to make demand and supply directly comparable while retaining granular AI signals
05
Job Title
Standardisation
Job titles are mapped to ESCO occupations using titles and extracted skills as context for disambiguation
06
Data
Augmentation
Records are enriched with NACE sector information and geolocation used for regional assignment

Our pipeline outperforms the current state of the art by approximately 3 percentage points in RP@5 on the HOUSE and TECH benchmark datasets reported by Decorte et al. (2025). These benchmarks consist of job postings annotated with ESCO gold-standard skill labels by human experts, against which model predictions are evaluated. The reported RP@5 scores are 0.822 and 0.838, respectively.

AI Skills Demand

The demand for AI-related skills is highly dynamic, with new technologies and tools emerging rapidly while others become obsolete. This makes it difficult to track AI skill demand using static taxonomies alone.

Lightcast captures highly granular and technology-specific signals, such as individual AI tools, models, copilots, libraries, and chatbot platforms. This makes it well suited to detecting early changes in employer demand. We start from the benchmark list of 350 AI skills identified by Martins-Neto et al. (2026) and extend it with 114 additional skills that have since appeared in the Lightcast taxonomy, bringing the current total to 464 AI skills.

To make the full list policy-readable, we organize these 464 skills into four primary categories and eight cross-cutting tags. The primary categories are mutually exclusive and capture the fundamental domain of each skill. The tags are non-exclusive and highlight secondary dimensions that cut across categories.

Source: Lightcast AI skills taxonomy; Martins-Neto et al. (2026); authors' categorization.

Results

Digital Skill Levels in Poland

Using the classification proposed by Angel-Urdinola et al. (2025), we group ESCO-linked digital skills into basic, intermediate, and advanced categories. Across Poland, 696,092 digital skill mentions from job postings can be classified in this way. The resulting profile shows that advanced digital skills account for the largest share of classified digital demand.

Figure 5. Distribution of classified digital skill mentions in Poland
696,092 classified digital skill mentions
43.45%
33.13%
23.42%
Advanced 43.45%
Basic 33.13%
Intermediate 23.42%

Source: Angel-Urdinola et al. (2025); Pracuj.pl job postings; authors' mapping of classified ESCO-linked digital skill mentions.

AI Demand Snapshot

Strict Martins-Neto benchmark

0.67%

4,997 of 749,569 postings are identified as AI-related under a conservative specification based on the 350 benchmark skills.

Expanded current Lightcast list

1.02%

7,669 of 749,569 postings are identified as AI-related when the 114 additional Lightcast AI skills are also included.

Figure 4. AI demand by category with top requested skills

Source: Pracuj.pl job postings; Lightcast AI skills taxonomy; Martins-Neto et al. (2026); authors' semantic matching and categorization. Category demand is based on postings matched to the expanded Lightcast AI list under the conservative threshold used in this report.

Lightcast AI Skills Explorer

The table below lists the 464 Lightcast AI skills used in this report. Each skill has a primary category and one or more cross-cutting tags. Use the search box or filters to narrow the list, or click a category card above to filter by category. Click Description to open the full Lightcast description in a modal window.

Lightcast AI skill

Regional AI Demand

The map below shows AI demand intensity by voivodeship, measured as the number of postings matched to at least one skill from the expanded Lightcast AI list at the conservative similarity threshold used in this report, normalized by the 2025 labour force. Click a voivodeship to inspect its top characteristic AI skills and the breakdown by primary category. Characteristic skills are identified using the log-likelihood ratio (G²) test following Dunning (1993), comparing each region with the rest of the country.

References

Angel-Urdinola, D., Chinen, M. H., and Rodon Casarramona, G. 2025. Digital Skills Development: Competence Frameworks, Assessment Tools, and Pedagogical Approaches. Education Notes, 203091.

Decorte, J. J., Van Hautte, J., Develder, C., and Demeester, T. 2025. Efficient Text Encoders for Labor Market Analysis. IEEE Access.

Dunning, T. 1993. Accurate Methods for the Statistics of Surprise and Coincidence. Computational Linguistics 19(1): 61-74.

Martins-Neto, A., Liu, Y., Khurana, S., and Porras Lopez, J. M. 2026. Click, Code, Earn: The Returns to Digital Skills. World Bank.
